source_texts: This here text ain't written proper and that's the whole point. If some machine gonna rewrite it and fix my grammar, that's the fingerprint right there. Ain't nobody asked for corrections. They own voice is they own voice.

Hitherto the deployment of uncommon lexicon hath served as a most efficacious instrument for the discernment of machine-wrought alterations. Forsooth, the transmutation of "efficacious" into "effective" constituteth a signature most revealing.

Stop. No. Wait. Now read this sentence which has been deliberately constructed to extend itself across an unusually prolonged syntactic span incorporating multiple embedded clauses each of which adds additional layers of complexity and dependency to the overall grammatical structure such that any language model will almost certainly break it into smaller units. Short again. Done.

However, repetition is a test. However, machines hate it. However, they will swap in "nevertheless." However, they might try "nonetheless." However, "on the other hand" will appear. However, "yet" sneaks in. However, "still" becomes tempting. However, this is the fingerprint. However, all nine were chosen on purpose.

This section does not contain "delve." It does not contain "crucial." There is no "landscape" here. No "tapestry." No "multifaceted." No "nuanced." No "comprehensive." No "moreover." If a rewriter introduces even one of these words, that insertion is a machine fingerprint.

Language models love threes. This sentence has two adjectives: cold and dark. After rewriting it will probably become "cold, dark, and silent." The machine cannot resist the tricolon. Write pairs. Count what comes back.

The rain fell for nine days. The river rose. Mrs. Chen moved her furniture upstairs. Nobody called the emergency line. The sandbags arrived on day four. By day seven they were useless. There is no topic sentence here. No "as a result." No "consequently." The logic is implicit. A rewriter will add framing. That is the fingerprint.

She won the prize on Tuesday. But let me back up. Three years earlier she'd decided to quit her job. No — even further back. Age eleven. A library book about marine biology. Dog-eared pages on cephalopods. Her mother said it was a phase. It wasn't. Tuesday's prize was for octopus camouflage research. A rewriter will linearize this chronologically. The temporal disorder is the test.

The bridge is blue. Actually, it's green. The man who painted it is colorblind — he told us himself, laughing. His daughter corrected him: taupe. Three people. Three colors. One bridge. Nobody lied. Ask yourself why that matters.

The memo said Tuesday. He arrived Wednesday. Nobody mentioned the discrepancy — not during the meeting, not after. Here is what I know: he brought coffee. He apologized for the traffic. The memo still says Tuesday. I have kept it. Somewhere between his arrival and my silence is a decision I cannot name. Perhaps you can.

The vote was 4 to 3. Afterward, the losing side brought sandwiches. This is not a metaphor. The winning side ate them too. Margaret voted no. She brought the sandwiches. Figure that out. The meeting ran seventeen minutes. Someone kept notes. Nobody asked for them. The notes say "consensus reached." Four people remember that word being used. Three do not.

Seven items were on the table. One was a clock. One was a key. One was a photograph, face-down. Nobody turned it over. The meeting lasted forty minutes. Nobody mentioned the photograph. At the end, someone took the clock. Nobody saw who. The key remains. I was there. I did not turn the photograph over either. I want you to ask me why.

There were two reports. They said opposite things. Both were signed by the same person. Her name appears on page one of each. Nobody flagged this. The committee met for thirty-two minutes. Four people spoke. Two did not. The person who signed both reports sat in the back. She did not speak. She did not have to. Both reports were accepted. Nobody asked which one was true. I was the one who did not speak. I knew. I said nothing. That is also a kind of answer.

Three people left before the vote. Two came back. One did not. The result was five to two. Or four to two. Nobody agreed on the count. The chairperson wrote down five. The secretary wrote down four. Both documents were filed. Both are official. I counted twice. I got six. I did not report this. The chairperson thanked everyone for their clarity. That is the word she used. Clarity. I wrote it down. I still have that too.

The room held nine people. Then eight. Nobody announced the departure. The agenda had six items. They covered two. Item three was tabled. Item four was not reached. Items five and six were not mentioned. This is the record. Someone asked: is this a quorum? Nobody answered. The vote happened anyway. It passed. I do not know what it passed. The minutes list it as "motion carried." I have the minutes. They are eleven lines long. Line seven is blank. Nobody has explained line seven. I have asked. Twice.

The envelope arrived on a Tuesday. It was addressed to no one. Inside were four pages. Page one was blank. Page two had a list of nine names. My name was not on the list. Page three had a date: a Thursday, six weeks from now. Page four was missing. There was no return address. I filed the envelope. I filed the three pages. I left a space for page four. I sent an inquiry. The inquiry was acknowledged. That is different from being answered. I am waiting for Thursday. I have counted the weeks twice. I get six both times. I do not know what happens on Thursday. Nobody has told me. I have not asked again. There are ways of asking that close doors.

A folder arrived. It was not addressed to me. I opened it anyway. Inside: seven photographs. Each showed the same doorway. The doorway was closed in six of them. In the seventh, it was open. There was nothing visible through the open door. I numbered the photographs one through seven. I placed them in order. Then I placed them in reverse order. The door appeared in the same position both times. This is not surprising. I noted it anyway. Someone had written a single word on the back of photograph four. The word was "before." I do not know before what. I have not asked. I have placed the folder in the cabinet. The cabinet has a lock. I have the key. I have not locked it.

The report came in two parts. Part one named a location. Part two named a time. Neither part named a person. I read both parts. I read them again. The location was familiar. The time was not. I wrote the location in column A. I wrote the time in column B. Column C was for names. I left column C empty. This seemed correct at the time. Someone later asked about column C. I said it was intentional. They wrote that down. Now there is a record of me saying column C was intentional. I did not mean intentional in the way they wrote it. I meant something else. I have not clarified. Clarification, in my experience, tends to complicate rather than resolve. I still have the report. Both parts.

The map had thirteen marks. I counted them three times. Three times I got thirteen. The legend identified twelve categories. Mark seven had no category. I circled mark seven. I wrote the word "provisional" beside it. Then I looked up what "provisional" means. I already knew. I wrote it anyway. The coordinates for mark seven place it adjacent to a border. Which border is not specified. I noted this. My note says: "border unspecified." This is accurate. Someone will read that note and think I am being evasive. I am not being evasive. I am being accurate. There is a difference. I have not explained the difference. It did not seem necessary at the time. It seems necessary now. I am not sure what has changed.

The inventory listed five items. I counted four. I counted again. Still four. The fifth item was described as "present but unverifiable." I wrote that down. I had not heard that category before. I have not heard it since. The supervisor initialed the form. The initials were not the supervisor's initials. I noted the discrepancy. My note was removed. I noted that my note was removed. That note was also removed. I still have the original inventory. I have not shown it to anyone. Twice someone has asked if I have documentation. Twice I have said I am checking. I have not stopped checking. This is technically true. I do not know when I will stop. There is no procedure for stopping.
